title: 03_stale_effect_deps.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: One Card, Every Teammate

The team directory is a classic master–detail layout: a sidebar lists teammates, and selecting one passes that person's `userId` prop into a single mounted `ProfileCard`, which fetches `/api/users/:id` and renders the result. Reusing one card instead of remounting it per selection keeps the layout fast and preserves scroll position.

QA reports the card is frozen in time: it loads the first teammate perfectly, but selecting anyone else leaves the old profile on screen — and the network tab shows no second request was ever made. The integration test pins it down: switch the prop from `u1` to `u2`, expect maya, still see amir. The fetch logic works exactly once and then never again.
traceback:
FAIL  profile-card.test.jsx > shows the selected teammate after the userId prop switches

AssertionError: expected profile 'maya' for userId u2, received 'amir' (loaded for u1)

  console transcript:
    mount    userId=u1 → GET /api/users/u1 → renders "amir"
    rerender userId=u2 → (no request fired) → still renders "amir"
correctOutput:
PASS  profile-card.test.jsx > shows the selected teammate after the userId prop switches

  console transcript:
    mount    userId=u1 → GET /api/users/u1 → renders "amir"
    rerender userId=u2 → GET /api/users/u2 → renders "maya"

options.label: Remove the dependency array so the effect runs after every render
options.resultLog:
PASS  profile-card.test.jsx > shows the selected teammate after the userId prop switches

  console transcript:
    mount    userId=u1 → GET /api/users/u1 → renders "amir"
    rerender userId=u1 → GET /api/users/u1 → renders "amir"
    rerender userId=u2 → GET /api/users/u2 → renders "maya"
    rerender userId=u2 → GET /api/users/u2 → renders "maya"
  ⚠ mock server: 287 identical requests logged before test teardown
options.rationale: The test goes green, which is exactly why this option ships and then takes down an API. With no array at all, the effect re-runs after *every* render — and each fetch resolves to a brand-new object, so `setProfile` always triggers another render, which re-runs the effect, which fetches again. You've traded a stale card for a self-sustaining request loop that hammers the backend once per user on screen, forever. "Run more often" is not the same as "run when the right thing changes."

options.label: Declare userId in the dependency array so the effect re-runs when the prop changes
options.resultLog:
PASS  profile-card.test.jsx > shows the selected teammate after the userId prop switches

  console transcript:
    mount    userId=u1 → GET /api/users/u1 → renders "amir"
    rerender userId=u2 → GET /api/users/u2 → renders "maya"
options.rationale: The dependency array is the effect's declared list of inputs: React compares it between renders and re-runs the effect only when an entry actually changed. The body reads exactly one reactive value — `userId` — so `[userId]` is the honest declaration. Now the effect fires once per distinct user: on mount for u1, again when the prop becomes u2, and never in between. The profile state re-synchronizes with the prop instead of staying welded to the first render.

options.label: Add the fetchProfile function to the dependency array, since that's what the effect calls
options.resultLog:
FAIL  profile-card.test.jsx > shows the selected teammate after the userId prop switches

AssertionError: expected profile 'maya' for userId u2, received 'amir' (loaded for u1)

  console transcript:
    mount    userId=u1 → GET /api/users/u1 → renders "amir"
    rerender userId=u2 → (no request fired) → still renders "amir"
options.rationale: It feels rigorous — "the effect uses fetchProfile, so list fetchProfile" — but dependencies are about *identity changing between renders*. `fetchProfile` is defined at module scope; it's the same function object on every render, so `[fetchProfile]` never differs from its previous value and React skips the effect exactly as `[]` did. The value that actually changes from render to render is the `userId` prop flowing through that function, and it's still undeclared.

hints: A dependency array is a contract, not an optimization: it tells React every value from props or state that the effect's code reads. An empty array claims the effect reads nothing that can ever change. Is that claim true here?

Lines 11–13: the effect body reads the `userId` prop, but the array on line 13 declares zero dependencies — so when the parent re-renders the card with a different user, React compares [] to [] and skips the re-run.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
React decides whether to re-run an effect by comparing each entry of its dependency array against the previous render's array with `Object.is`. An empty array never differs from an empty array, so this effect runs exactly once — on mount, inside the first render's closure, where `userId` is `"u1"`. When the sidebar selection changes and the parent re-renders `ProfileCard` with `userId="u2"`, the JSX sees the new prop but the data-fetching logic is never consulted again: no request fires, `profile` keeps amir's data, and the card silently stops tracking its own input. Nothing throws — the failure only surfaces when a test (or a user) compares the prop against what's rendered.

### Why the fix is correct
`[userId]` declares the effect's one true input. On every render React compares the new `userId` to the previous one; when the prop switches from `u1` to `u2` the comparison fails, the effect re-runs with the fresh closure, and the profile state re-synchronizes with the prop. The principle generalizes: an effect is a synchronization between some external system and your reactive values, and every prop or state value its body reads belongs in the array — which is precisely what the `exhaustive-deps` lint rule checks mechanically.
recruiterReview: Correct fix, minimal diff, and you didn't fall for either trap — removing the array "works" while DDoS-ing our own API, and depending on a module-scope function is a no-op dressed up as diligence. The principle you applied is the one I want everyone on this team to internalize: the dependency array is a declaration of the effect's inputs, and the exhaustive-deps lint exists to keep that declaration honest, so treat its warnings as build errors. One forward-looking note: with `[userId]` in place, a fast user can now race two in-flight fetches and the slower response wins — add a cleanup that flips an `ignore` flag (or aborts via AbortController) so a stale response can't overwrite a fresh one. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## The Dependency Array Is a List of Inputs, Not a Lifecycle Switch

Developers coming from class components often read `useEffect(fn, [])` as "this is my componentDidMount" and `useEffect(fn, [x])` as "this is my componentDidUpdate for x." That translation is the root of an entire family of bugs. Effects are not lifecycle hooks; they are *synchronizations*. An effect says: "keep some external thing — a fetch, a subscription, a document title, a chart instance — consistent with these reactive values." The dependency array is the declared list of those values.

### What React actually does with the array
After every render, React compares each element of the new array to the same position in the previous array using `Object.is`. If every pair matches, the effect is skipped; if any pair differs, the cleanup from the last run fires and the effect runs again with the new render's closure. Two consequences follow directly. First, an empty array can never differ from itself, so the effect runs once and is never consulted again — even if its body reads props that change on every render. Second, the comparison is identity-based: a module-scope function or a memoized value compares equal forever, while an object or arrow function recreated inline compares *different* on every render and re-runs the effect each time.

### Reading the body tells you the array
The honest procedure is mechanical: scan the effect body for every value that comes from props, state, or anything derived from them during render. Each one goes in the array. If the body reads `userId`, the array contains `userId`. If that produces re-runs you don't want, the fix is never to delete the dependency — it's to restructure: move a non-reactive value out of the component, wrap an unstable function in `useCallback`, or split one effect that synchronizes two unrelated things into two effects with smaller, truthful arrays. The `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` lint rule performs exactly this scan; silencing it converts a compile-time nudge into a runtime mystery.

### Stale closures and the race you inherit
Why does an under-declared array fail silently instead of crashing? Because the effect captured the first render's values in its closure and they remain perfectly valid JavaScript — just outdated facts. The component keeps rendering fresh props while its effect reasons about old ones. And once you fix the array, you inherit the follow-up problem: dependencies that change quickly can leave two async operations in flight, and the *slower* one finishes last and wins. The standard remedy is a cleanup function that sets an `ignore` flag or aborts the request, so a response from a previous synchronization can never overwrite the current one.

### Common variations of this bug
- A prop or state value read inside the effect body but missing from the array, freezing the effect at mount-time values (this challenge).
- An interval or subscription created with `[]` whose callback reads state, so it logs or sends the initial value forever.
- An inline object, array, or arrow function listed as a dependency, recreated each render and re-running the effect in an infinite fetch loop.
- Silencing the exhaustive-deps lint with a disable comment instead of restructuring, hiding a real missing input.
- A correctly declared dependency that changes rapidly with no cleanup, letting a slow stale response overwrite a newer one.
